---
title: "Properties of MLE: Uncertainty in Parameters"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 3"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Deck setup. Runs from the project root (execute-dir: project), so the shared
# helpers import the same way from any module depth.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "shared")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import slide_helpers as sh

sh.use_slide_style()

# >>> PLACEHOLDER — replace with the deployed parameter-uncertainty tool URL. <<<
# Single source of truth for the live-demo iframe AND its fallback caption.
PLACEHOLDER_URL = "https://gchism94.github.io/info521-activities-2026/week03-bayesian-updating/"
```

## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of this module you should be able to:

1. **Show the MLE solution is unique** — that the log-likelihood has a single
   maximum (a negative-definite Hessian).
2. **State what unbiasedness of $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ does and does not mean** — and
   the common claims it is *not*.
3. **Quantify parameter uncertainty** — via $\mathrm{cov}\{\hat{\mathbf{w}}\}$ and
   the Fisher information.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: three properties, one per outcome — uniqueness, unbiasedness,
uncertainty. The checkpoint gates on reproducing the first two arguments.
:::

## Recap: likelihood and the MLE {.smaller}

The linear-Gaussian likelihood:

$$
p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)
\;=\; \prod_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_n \,\middle|\, \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n,\ \sigma^2\right)
$$

Its maximizers — the MLE we derived last module:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y},
\qquad
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \tfrac{1}{N}\left(\mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{w}}\right)
$$

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: we established these last module; today the question is — how good
is $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$? Is it unique, is it unbiased, and how uncertain is it?
:::

# Unique, unbiased, and how uncertain. {.center}

## Property 1 · Uniqueness {.smaller}

The log-likelihood — we write it $\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \log p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)$,
reserving $\mathcal{L}$ for losses:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\tfrac{N}{2}\log 2\pi \;-\; N\log\sigma
\;-\; \tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left(y_n - \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n\right)^2
$$

::: {.fragment}
Gradient — vanishes at the normal equations:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mathbf{w}}
= \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\right)
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
Hessian — constant and **negative definite** ($\mathbf{x}^{\!\top}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} < 0$):

$$
\mathbf{H} \;=\; \frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \mathbf{w}\,\partial \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}}
\;=\; -\tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{a single, unique maximum.}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
[The same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ full-column-rank condition from Module 1 —
now reappearing as a negative-definite Hessian.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: negative-definite everywhere ⇒ strictly concave ⇒ at most one
stationary point, and it is the global max. Ties uniqueness to rank of X.
:::

## The generative picture

```{=html}
<!-- Redrawn schematic (NOT a data figure): the generating process produces
     datasets; refitting across repeated datasets gives a SPREAD in w-hat.
     Stroke/text inherit currentColor (=> theme text); the spread + ellipse use
     the accent token. Revealed as reveal fragments. -->
<svg viewBox="0 0 980 430" width="100%" style="max-height:64vh; color: var(--text); font-family: inherit;"
     role="img" aria-label="Schematic: a generating process with true weights w and noise sigma-squared produces a dataset; fitting it gives one estimate w-hat. Repeating over many datasets yields a cloud of estimates whose spread is summarized by a covariance ellipse.">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7" refY="4.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L9,4.5 L0,9 z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Stage 1: generating process -->
  <g class="fragment" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" stroke-width="1.6">
    <rect x="20" y="150" width="230" height="110" rx="10"/>
    <text x="135" y="120" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" font-weight="650">generating process</text>
    <text x="135" y="195" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20">y&#8345; = w&#7488;x&#8345; + &#949;&#8345;</text>
    <text x="135" y="228" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17">&#949;&#8345; ~ N(0, &#963;&#178;)</text>
    <text x="135" y="287" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" opacity="0.8">true w, &#963;&#178; (fixed, unknown)</text>
  </g>

  <!-- Stage 2: one dataset -> one estimate -->
  <g class="fragment" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" stroke-width="1.6">
    <line x1="250" y1="205" x2="345" y2="205" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
    <rect x="350" y="165" width="180" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text x="440" y="200" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18">dataset D</text>
    <text x="440" y="226" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" opacity="0.8">N draws</text>
    <line x1="530" y1="205" x2="625" y2="205" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
    <text x="578" y="193" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" opacity="0.85">fit</text>
    <rect x="630" y="170" width="120" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text x="690" y="212" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20">&#373; = (X&#7488;X)&#8315;&#185;X&#7488;y</text>
  </g>

  <!-- Stage 3: repeat over datasets -> cloud of estimates -->
  <g class="fragment">
    <g stroke="currentColor" fill="none" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.55">
      <rect x="362" y="153" width="180" height="80" rx="10"/>
      <rect x="374" y="141" width="180" height="80" rx="10"/>
    </g>
    <text x="468" y="128" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" opacity="0.85">repeated datasets D&#185;, D&#178;, &#8230;</text>
    <g style="fill: var(--accent);" stroke="none">
      <circle cx="820" cy="150" r="4"/><circle cx="852" cy="168" r="4"/>
      <circle cx="835" cy="192" r="4"/><circle cx="868" cy="150" r="4"/>
      <circle cx="806" cy="178" r="4"/><circle cx="845" cy="138" r="4"/>
      <circle cx="880" cy="178" r="4"/><circle cx="824" cy="205" r="4"/>
      <circle cx="860" cy="205" r="4"/><circle cx="888" cy="160" r="4"/>
    </g>
    <line x1="752" y1="205" x2="795" y2="190" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.6" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
    <text x="848" y="118" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" opacity="0.85">each D &#8594; its own &#373;</text>
  </g>

  <!-- Stage 4: spread summarized by covariance ellipse -->
  <g class="fragment">
    <ellipse cx="847" cy="175" rx="62" ry="44" transform="rotate(-28 847 175)"
             fill="none" style="stroke: var(--accent);" stroke-width="2.4" stroke-dasharray="7 5"/>
    <text x="847" y="258" stroke="none" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16"
          style="fill: var(--accent);" font-weight="650">spread of &#373; = cov{&#373;}</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: cross-checked against source slides on the generating picture, but
redrawn clean in theme colors. The punchline is the last fragment: uncertainty in
$\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ is the spread of estimates across repeated datasets.
:::

## Expectation — a reminder {.smaller}

<!-- CUTTABLE: prereq recap (async L09a). First slide to drop if the deck runs
     long; nothing downstream depends on it. -->

::: {style="border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:10px; padding:0.4em 1em; background:var(--surface);"}
For a random vector $\mathbf{x}$ with density $p(\mathbf{x})$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{x})}\{f(\mathbf{x})\} = \int f(\mathbf{x})\,p(\mathbf{x})\,d\mathbf{x}
$$

**Mean** $\;\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}]\qquad$
**Covariance** $\;\mathrm{cov}\{\mathbf{x}\} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\!\top}\right]$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: prereq recap (async L09a) — keep brief. We only need linearity of
expectation and the covariance definition for the next two slides.
:::

## Property 2 · Unbiasedness {.smaller}

Take the expectation of $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ over datasets from the generating
process (only $\mathbf{y}$ is random; $\mathbf{X}$ is fixed):

::: {.fragment}
$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}]
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
= \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
= \mathbf{w} \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \hat{\mathbf{w}}\ \text{is unbiased.}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: uses $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}] = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$ (the model mean).
The estimator is centered on the true $\mathbf{w}$ — on average across datasets.
:::

## What "unbiased" means — and doesn't {.smaller}

**Unbiased means:** across repeated size-$N$ samples from the generating
process, the collection of estimates $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ is **centered** on the
true $\mathbf{w}$.

It is **not** the same as:

- **"more data ⇒ better estimates"** — true here, but that is a *separate*
  sample-size effect, not what unbiasedness asserts;
- **UMVU** (uniformly minimum-variance unbiased) — a strictly stronger claim
  about being *closest*, which unbiasedness alone does not give;
- a guarantee of being best for *your* dataset — a **biased** estimator can land
  closer to $\mathbf{w}$ from *less* data.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this is the slide students misremember — keep the three "not"s
crisp. Unbiasedness is a statement about the average, not any single fit.
:::

## Property 3 · Covariance

The punchline first — the parameter covariance is

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{cov}\{\hat{\mathbf{w}}\} \;=\; \sigma^2\left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\;}
$$

Derived in full in the appendix → [appendix-covariance.html](appendix-covariance.html).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: small $\sigma^2$ (low noise) or large/well-conditioned
$\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ (more, more-spread data) ⇒ tighter estimates. The
full $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}\hat{\mathbf{w}}^{\!\top}]$ chain is the appendix.
:::

## Fisher information {.smaller}

The (expected) Fisher information is the curvature of the log-likelihood:

$$
\mathcal{I} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[-\,\frac{\partial^2 \log p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)}{\partial \mathbf{w}\,\partial \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}}\right]
\;=\; \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\mathrm{cov}\{\hat{\mathbf{w}}\} \;=\; \mathcal{I}^{-1}
$$

- **Diagonal** $\mathcal{I}_{jj}$ — information about each parameter on its own;
- **Off-diagonal** $\mathcal{I}_{jk}$ — information *shared* between a pair (their
  estimates trade off).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: more information ⇒ smaller inverse ⇒ tighter covariance. Note the
Hessian from Property 1 reappears here — same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$.
:::

## Parameter uncertainty on NHANES

```{python}
#| label: fig-cov-ellipses
#| fig-alt: "Two panels. Left: scatter of systolic BP against standardized age for NHANES adults in blue with a vermillion least-squares fit line. Right: the parameter plane of intercept w0 versus slope w1, showing two 2-sigma covariance ellipses — a wide dashed orange ellipse for a small sample of N=40 and a much smaller solid green ellipse for the full sample, both centered on the full-data estimate, illustrating that more data gives a tighter parameter covariance."
#| fig-cap: "cov{ŵ} = σ̂²(XᵀX)⁻¹ shrinks as N grows: N = 40 (orange, dashed) vs. full N (green, solid)."

ds  = sh.load_nhanes()
age = sh.primary_predictor(ds)
y   = ds["y"]

# Standardize the single feature so w = [w0 (intercept), w1 (slope)] is 2-D.
z     = (age - age.mean()) / age.std()
Xfull = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(z), z])

def mle_fit(X, yv):
    """ŵ, σ̂², cov{ŵ} per the Module-3 formulas (all from scratch)."""
    w   = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ yv)
    N   = len(yv)
    s2  = (yv @ yv - yv @ (X @ w)) / N        # σ̂² = (1/N)(yᵀy − yᵀXŵ)
    cov = s2 * np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)         # cov{ŵ} = σ̂²(XᵀX)⁻¹
    return w, s2, cov

w_full, _, cov_full = mle_fit(Xfull, y)

rng = np.random.default_rng(521)              # fixed seed: reproducible figure
idx = rng.choice(len(y), size=40, replace=False)
w_small, _, cov_small = mle_fit(Xfull[idx], y[idx])

def ellipse(mean, cov, nsig=2.0, n=240):
    vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(cov)
    t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, n)
    pts = vecs @ (np.sqrt(vals)[:, None] * nsig * np.stack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t)]))
    return mean[0] + pts[0], mean[1] + pts[1]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.2, 4.7))

# Left: data + least-squares fit.
order = np.argsort(z)
axL.scatter(z, y, s=10, alpha=0.18, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], label="NHANES adults")
axL.plot(z[order], (Xfull @ w_full)[order], color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"],
         linestyle="-", label="least-squares fit")
axL.set_xlabel("standardized age (z)")
axL.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)

# Right: the two covariance ellipses on the (w0, w1) plane (centered at ŵ_full
# so the SIZE difference is the message). Paired cues: color + line style + marker.
exs, eys = ellipse(w_full, cov_small)
exl, eyl = ellipse(w_full, cov_full)
axR.plot(exs, eys, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"], linestyle="--",
         marker="s", markevery=30, label="N = 40")
axR.plot(exl, eyl, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["green"], linestyle="-",
         marker="o", markevery=30, label="N = full")
axR.plot(w_full[0], w_full[1], marker="+", ms=12, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["purple"])
axR.set_xlabel(r"$w_0$  (intercept)")
axR.set_ylabel(r"$w_1$  (slope)")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False)

sh.finalize(fig, alt="NHANES least-squares fit and the N=40 vs full-N covariance ellipses for the fitted parameters.")
plt.show()
```

## Live demo: parameter-uncertainty explorer

::: {style="color: var(--accent); font-weight:650;"}
Pipeline test — placeholder tool; the parameter-uncertainty viz is a future M3 build.
:::

```{python}
#| label: live-iframe
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML
HTML(
    f'<iframe src="{PLACEHOLDER_URL}" title="Parameter-uncertainty explorer" '
    'width="100%" height="430" loading="lazy" '
    'style="border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:8px; background:var(--surface);">'
    '</iframe>'
)
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: placeholder iframe — swap `PLACEHOLDER_URL` (top of deck) when the
M3 uncertainty tool ships. The static fallback (next slide) is what prints.
:::

## Explorer — static fallback {.smaller}

*The iframe above does not print or export to PDF; this snapshot stands in.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-fallback
#| fig-alt: "Static snapshot standing in for the interactive explorer: the parameter plane of intercept w0 versus slope w1 with the small-sample (N=40, orange dashed) and full-sample (green solid) covariance ellipses, the same view a parameter-uncertainty tool would show."
#| fig-cap: "Static fallback: the covariance ellipses (used in print / PDF)."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.6, 4.6))
ax.plot(exs, eys, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"], linestyle="--",
        marker="s", markevery=30, label="N = 40")
ax.plot(exl, eyl, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["green"], linestyle="-",
        marker="o", markevery=30, label="N = full")
ax.plot(w_full[0], w_full[1], marker="+", ms=12, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["purple"])
ax.set_xlabel(r"$w_0$  (intercept)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$w_1$  (slope)")
ax.set_title("parameter-uncertainty snapshot (placeholder)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Static fallback snapshot of the parameter covariance ellipses.")
plt.show()
```

## Through-line {.smaller}

::: {style="color: var(--accent); font-weight:650;"}
Uniqueness (negative-definite Hessian) → Fisher information $\mathcal{I} = \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ → (Module 5) the Laplace approximation's posterior covariance $\mathbf{H}^{-1} = \mathcal{I}^{-1}$.
:::

The curvature of the log-likelihood is one object doing three jobs, three modules
apart — **same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$, same inverse.**

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: plant this now; in Module 5 the Laplace posterior covariance is
literally this $\mathcal{I}^{-1}$. Students should feel the callback land.
:::

## Recap + checkpoint preview {.smaller}

Three properties of the linear-Gaussian MLE:

1. **Unique** — $\mathbf{H} = -\tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ is negative definite.
2. **Unbiased** — $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] = \mathbf{w}$.
3. **Uncertain, quantifiably** — $\mathrm{cov}\{\hat{\mathbf{w}}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \mathcal{I}^{-1}$.

The **P1-M2 closed-book checkpoint** gates on reproducing the **uniqueness** and
**unbiasedness** arguments from scratch.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: bridge to the checkpoint. Students should be able to reproduce the
Hessian sign argument and the three-line unbiasedness derivation unaided.
:::
